planting.note: Plant the pseudobulbs in spring once hard frost has passed.

directions: Set the corm-like pseudobulbs about two inches deep in humus-rich, well-drained soil with the growing tip pointing up. Keep evenly moist through the growing season and mulch the crown in colder zones for winter protection. Pleated leaves emerge in spring and arching sprays of magenta-purple flowers open in late spring to early summer.

planting.note: Plant pseudobulbs in spring after the last frost.

directions: Plant the pseudobulbs two inches deep in rich, moisture-retentive soil that drains freely. Provide dappled shade in hot climates and keep the soil from drying out while the plant is in leaf. Pure white flowers with a lightly frilled lip appear on wiry stems in late spring.

planting.note: Plant pseudobulbs in spring as growth resumes.

directions: Bury the pseudobulbs a couple inches deep in loose, organic soil in a sheltered, part-shaded bed. Water regularly during active growth and reduce moisture after the foliage yellows in autumn. Soft primrose-yellow flowers, often flushed maroon on the lip, open a little later than the common purple species.

planting.note: Plant pseudobulbs in spring after frost danger passes.

directions: Plant the pseudobulbs two inches deep in fertile, free-draining soil enriched with leaf mold. Keep consistently moist while in leaf and shelter from strong afternoon sun. This vigorous hybrid carries larger, bicolored flowers in soft pink and cream from late spring into early summer.

planting.note: Plant dormant crowns in autumn in a limestone-rich bed.

directions: Nestle the rhizome an inch deep in gritty, alkaline soil amended with limestone chippings and leaf mold. Provide cool roots, morning sun, and afternoon shade. Yellow pouches with maroon spiraling petals appear in late spring on this classic European species.

planting.note: Plant dormant crowns in autumn in rich bottomland soil.

directions: Plant the rhizome an inch deep in deep, humus-rich soil that stays evenly moist, mimicking a streamside floodplain. Give filtered light and protect the emerging shoots from late frosts. The largest of the native slippers bears creamy ivory pouches in late spring.

planting.note: Plant dormant rhizomes in autumn in cool woodland soil.

directions: Set the rhizome about one inch deep in loose, organic, free-draining soil with a cool root run. This is one of the easier lady's slippers for the shade garden and clumps up steadily. Pale pink pouches spotted with deeper rose open above pleated, fan-shaped leaves in spring.

planting.note: Plant dormant rhizomes in autumn in deep shade.

directions: Lay the running rhizome an inch below rich, leafy soil in a cool, sheltered, shaded spot. Keep the root zone moist but never waterlogged and top-dress annually with leaf mold. Broad, fan-pleated leaves frame a single pink-tinged pouch in mid to late spring.

planting.note: Plant dormant divisions in autumn in a humus-rich bed.

directions: Plant the rhizome an inch deep in free-draining, humus-rich soil with a cool root run and morning light. This robust garden hybrid multiplies readily and is among the easiest slippers for beginners. Yellow pouches set against wine-red twisted petals open in late spring.

planting.note: Plant dormant crowns in autumn in cool, moist soil.

directions: Set the roots just under the surface in cool, evenly moist, neutral soil rich in leaf mold. This vigorous hybrid tolerates damp ground well and forms generous clumps over time. Soft white pouches flushed with pale pink open in early summer.

planting.note: Pot the pseudobulbs in late winter to early spring as growth begins.

directions: Nestle each pseudobulb with its top third above a gritty, bark-based compost in a shallow pan or sheltered raised bed. Keep just moist as roots form, then water freely in leaf and dry off after the foliage dies back. Solitary pink flowers with a fringed, spotted lip open in early spring, often before the leaf expands.

planting.note: Pot pseudobulbs in early spring as new roots appear.

directions: Press the pseudobulbs into an open, free-draining mix so the upper portion sits proud of the surface. Water sparingly until roots establish, then keep moist through the growing season and rest dry over winter. Rose-purple flowers with a paler, blotched lip open in spring alongside the emerging leaf.

planting.note: Pot pseudobulbs in late winter as growth resumes.

directions: Set the pseudobulbs shallowly in a bark and grit compost with the shoulder exposed, in bright but shaded light. Water freely once in leaf and give a dry, cool rest through winter. This popular grex bears large soft yellow to peach flowers with a boldly marked lip in early spring.

planting.note: Pot pseudobulbs in early spring before leaves expand.

directions: Plant each pseudobulb with two-thirds buried in a gritty, humus-rich mix in a shallow container or sheltered pocket. Keep barely moist until active, then water regularly and dry off after leaf fall. Deep rose-pink flowers with a richly patterned lip open in spring.

planting.note: Plant pseudobulbs in early spring in a sheltered rock crevice.

directions: This is the hardiest Pleione and can be grown outdoors in a sharply drained, mossy pocket among rocks. Set pseudobulbs with the tips exposed and protect from winter wet with a pane or overhang. Vivid magenta-pink flowers with a crimson-spotted lip appear in spring and it slowly forms a colony.

planting.note: Plant rhizome divisions in spring in a damp, limy bed.

directions: Set the creeping rhizomes just below the surface in moist, alkaline, low-nutrient soil that never dries out. It naturalizes into a slow-spreading patch in a sunny bog or damp meadow. Delicate white-and-pink lipped flowers with maroon markings open in mid summer.

planting.note: Plant rhizome divisions in spring in moist woodland soil.

directions: Plant the vigorous rhizomes an inch deep in rich, reliably moist soil in light shade. This robust hybrid clumps and spreads more freely than the species and tolerates ordinary garden beds if kept damp. Tall stems carry many coppery-pink flowers over a long season in summer.

planting.note: Plant dormant tubers in autumn in a moist, humus-rich bed.

directions: Plant the palmate tubers two to three inches deep in cool, moisture-retentive, moderately acidic soil. Give bright but not scorching light and keep the ground damp through spring growth. Dense conical spikes of pale lilac to pink flowers with spotted leaves appear in early summer.

planting.note: Plant dormant tubers in autumn in rich, moist soil.

directions: Set the finger-like tubers three inches deep in fertile, humus-rich soil that stays evenly moist but drains well. One of the showiest and easiest marsh orchids for the border, it bulks up into a fine clump. Bold, densely packed spikes of deep magenta-pink flowers rise above glossy leaves in early summer.

planting.note: Plant dormant tubers in autumn in a damp meadow or bed.

directions: Plant the tubers a few inches deep in moist, neutral to alkaline soil rich in organic matter. It naturalizes readily in damp grass and light shade once settled. Pale pink to lilac flowers marked with darker loops form neat spikes above spotted foliage in early summer.

planting.note: Plant dormant tubers in autumn in consistently moist ground.

directions: Bury the tubers two to three inches deep in wet to moist, humus-rich soil at a bog or pond margin. Keep the ground reliably damp and avoid strong fertilizers. Stout spikes of rich purple-pink flowers rise above broad, often spotted leaves in late spring to early summer.

planting.note: Plant dormant tubers in autumn in deep, moist soil.

directions: Plant the tubers three inches deep in deep, fertile, moisture-retentive soil in sun to light shade. The tallest of the commonly grown marsh orchids, it makes an imposing clump in a damp border. Long, dense spikes of vivid purple-magenta flowers open in early summer.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in moist, sunny soil.

directions: Set the fleshy roots just below the surface in moist to wet, humus-rich soil in full sun to light shade. This selected clone is vigorous, sweetly vanilla-scented, and easy in a damp border or bog edge. Spiraled columns of small white flowers open from late summer into autumn.

planting.note: Set divisions out in spring or early fall with the crown at soil level.

directions: Plant the fan so the white base of the crown sits just at the soil surface and water in well. This compact gold rebloomer flowers from early summer until frost, so shear spent scapes to keep new buds coming. Divide clumps every three or four years to sustain heavy flowering.

planting.note: Set out in spring for bloom the same season in most gardens.

directions: Plant the fan with roots fanned out and the crown just below the surface. This compact rebloomer opens rich ruby-red flowers with a small yellow throat over a long season. Deadhead regularly and shear tired foliage to freshen the clump.

planting.note: Plant spring or early fall in full sun to bring out the bicolor contrast.

directions: Set the division at grade and water deeply as roots take hold. Blooms are boldly bicolored, with rust-orange petals striped yellow alternating with clear yellow sepals. Remove spent scapes after the midsummer flush to keep plants neat.

planting.note: Plant spring or fall; partial afternoon shade helps hold the pastel tone.

directions: Firm soil around the crown at grade and keep new plantings watered through the first summer. Tall scapes bear fragrant orchid-lavender flowers with a green-yellow throat. Deadhead daily for a cleaner look and cut scapes down once bloom ends.

planting.note: Plant in spring or early fall in full sun for the whitest flowers.

directions: Set the division with the crown just at the surface and water well to settle the roots. Near-white ruffled flowers with a faint green throat open in early to midsummer. Remove spent blooms and divide the clump when flowering thins.

planting.note: A small grower ideal for the front of a border; plant in spring.

directions: Plant the crown at grade and keep it evenly moist as it settles. This dwarf carries rose-red flowers with a green throat over a long midsummer season. Pinch off finished flowers and shear old scapes to tidy the plant.

planting.note: Plant spring to early summer for reblooming through the season.

directions: Fan the roots into the planting hole and keep the crown near the surface. This compact rebloomer opens purple flowers with a gold throat from early summer onward. Deadhead often to prolong the show and divide crowded clumps in fall.

planting.note: A tall, vigorous variety; give it space at the back of a bed.

directions: Set the crown at grade and water thoroughly to establish the strong root system. Tall scapes hold bright orange-tangerine flowers with a red blush and gold throat in midsummer. Cut scapes down after bloom and lift crowded clumps every few years.

planting.note: Plant in full sun; some afternoon shade helps the dark petals hold color.

directions: Plant the division with its crown at the soil surface and mulch to keep roots cool. Large deep purple flowers with a green-gold throat rise on strong scapes in midsummer. Remove spent blooms daily to showcase the dark color and cut scapes when done.

planting.note: Plant spring or fall in full sun for the best repeat bloom.

directions: Settle the crown at grade and water in well as roots knit into the soil. Strawberry-pink flowers with a rose-red eye and gold edge appear in early summer and rebloom. Deadhead and give a summer feeding to push the second flush.

planting.note: A tall, large-flowered variety; place at the back of the border.

directions: Give this vigorous grower room and set the crown at soil level in rich ground. Big spidery tangerine-orange flowers with twisted petals bloom in mid to late summer. Cut scapes back after flowering and divide when the clump crowds itself.

planting.note: Plant near a path or seating area to enjoy the scent.

directions: Plant the fan with the crown just below the surface and water regularly the first year. Fragrant lemon-yellow flowers rebloom from early summer into fall. Remove spent scapes to keep flushes of new buds forming.

planting.note: Plant in spring for reblooming color through summer and fall.

directions: Set the crown at grade and keep evenly moist while establishing. Golden-yellow flowers with a red eyezone rebloom over a long summer season. Shear spent scapes and old foliage to keep the compact clump fresh.

planting.note: Plant spring or fall in full sun for large early flowers.

directions: Position the crown at soil level and firm the soil around the roots. Large ruffled buff-yellow flowers open early in the daylily season. Deadhead daily and cut scapes to the base once blooming finishes.

planting.note: Plant in full sun; the pale flowers glow at dusk in the garden.

directions: Set the division at grade in well-drained soil and water in thoroughly. Near-white flowers with a green throat appear in early to midsummer. Remove finished blooms and cut scapes down when the flush ends.

planting.note: Plant spring or fall; a late-season bloomer worth waiting for.

directions: Plant the crown at grade and keep watered until the roots establish. Mustard-yellow flowers carry a wine-purple eyezone and matching picotee edge in late summer. Deadhead as flowers fade and cut scapes when bloom is over.

planting.note: Plant spring or fall in full sun; a heavy bloomer and good multiplier.

directions: Fan the roots out and set the crown just at the surface. Cream-yellow flowers with a maroon eyezone bloom heavily in early to midsummer. Deadhead regularly and divide every few years to keep bloom counts high.

planting.note: An old, reliable variety; plant spring or fall in full sun.

directions: Set the crown at grade and water deeply to establish. Rich red flowers with a yellow-green throat bloom from mid into late summer. Snap off spent flowers and cut scapes back once the season ends.

planting.note: A vigorous spreader; plant where it can naturalize freely.

directions: Plant divisions at grade in almost any soil; this tough old form spreads by runners. Double tawny-orange flowers open on tall scapes in midsummer. Contain the roots if you want to limit its spread and cut scapes after bloom.

planting.note: The classic roadside daylily; naturalizes readily in rough ground.

directions: Set divisions at grade in sun or light shade; this sterile clone spreads by roots rather than seed. Rusty-orange flowers appear on branched scapes in midsummer. Give it space or edge the planting to keep it in bounds.

planting.note: Plant spring or fall in full sun for the crisp eye contrast.

directions: Set the crown at grade and keep moist until established. Orchid-lavender flowers show a purple eyezone above a green throat in early to midsummer. Remove spent blooms and lift crowded clumps every few years.

planting.note: A dependable small variety; plant spring or early fall.

directions: Plant the crown at grade and water in well. Ruffled canary-yellow flowers with a strong green throat bloom in mid to late summer. Deadhead often and cut scapes to the base when finished.

planting.note: Afternoon shade helps preserve the lavender-blue tones.

directions: Set the crown at grade and keep evenly moist during establishment. Lavender flowers with a smoky blue-violet eye and green throat bloom in midsummer. Remove finished flowers and cut scapes after the flush.

planting.note: An award-winning pink; plant spring or fall in full sun.

directions: Fan the roots and set the crown just at the surface. Large ruffled soft-pink flowers with a green throat open in midsummer. Deadhead daily for a clean look and divide when clumps grow crowded.

planting.note: Plant spring or fall in full sun for well-formed, heavily ruffled flowers.

directions: Set the crown at grade and keep the soil moist while establishing. Pink flowers with a green throat and heavy ruffled edge bloom in midsummer. Remove spent blooms and cut scapes back when the season ends.

planting.note: A tall, vigorous grower; plant spring or fall in full sun.

directions: Set the crown at grade and water deeply as roots establish. Cream flowers edged and picoteed in rose-pink bloom on tall scapes in midsummer. Deadhead regularly and cut scapes down after bloom.

planting.note: Plant spring or fall; reblooms in warmer regions.

directions: Set the crown at grade and keep evenly watered while it settles. Mauve-lavender flowers with a deep purple eye and green throat bloom in early to midsummer. Deadhead to encourage repeat bloom where the season is long.

planting.note: A heavy bloomer and good multiplier; plant spring or fall.

directions: Fan the roots and set the crown just at the surface. Pink flowers with a rose band, yellow-green throat, and gold edge bloom over a long season and often rebloom. Deadhead often and divide every few years to sustain vigor.

planting.note: A dainty pastel; plant spring or fall near the front of a bed.

directions: Set the crown at grade and water in well. Ruffled pale pink flowers with a green throat bloom in early to midsummer. Remove spent flowers and cut scapes down when finished.

planting.note: A tall grower; plant spring or fall in full sun.

directions: Set the crown at grade and water deeply to establish. Large rose-cherry flowers with a lighter midrib and yellow-green throat bloom in midsummer. Deadhead daily and cut scapes to the base after bloom.

planting.note: A large, showy red; plant spring or fall in full sun.

directions: Give this vigorous plant room and set the crown at grade in rich soil. Large ruffled scarlet-red flowers with a gold throat bloom on tall scapes in midsummer. Remove spent flowers and divide when clumps become crowded.

planting.note: Reblooms in warmer zones; plant spring or fall in full sun.

directions: Set the crown at grade and keep moist while establishing. Coral to tangerine flowers with ruffled edges and a green throat bloom over a long season. Deadhead often to keep new buds forming.

planting.note: A dramatic large-flowered spider; give it room in the border.

directions: Set the crown at grade and water deeply as roots establish. Huge ruby-red spider flowers with a yellow throat open on tall scapes in early to midsummer. Deadhead spent blooms and cut scapes back after flowering.

planting.note: A fragrant double; plant spring or fall in full sun.

directions: Fan the roots and set the crown just at the surface. Fragrant double bubblegum-pink flowers with a green throat bloom in midsummer. Deadhead daily and divide the clump when flowering thins.

planting.note: Plant spring or fall in full sun for the darkest petals.

directions: Set the crown at grade and mulch to keep roots cool. Deep purple flowers with a yellow-green throat and gold-edged petals bloom in midsummer. Remove spent blooms daily and cut scapes when done.

planting.note: One of the whitest daylilies; plant spring or fall in full sun.

directions: Set the crown at grade and keep moist while establishing. Near-white flowers with a green throat open in early to midsummer. Deadhead daily to showcase the pale color and cut scapes after bloom.

planting.note: Grown as much for foliage; morning sun keeps variegation crisp.

directions: Set the crown at grade in soil that stays lightly moist. Foliage is boldly striped green and creamy white, topped by tawny-orange flowers in summer. Remove any all-green shoots that appear to keep the variegation strong.

planting.note: The naturalized ditch daylily; spreads by roots, not seed.

directions: Plant divisions at grade in almost any soil, in sun or light shade. This tough sterile species carries rusty-orange trumpets on tall branched scapes in midsummer and spreads by underground runners. Edge or contain it where you need to limit its vigorous spread.

planting.note: Start seed indoors in late winter for bloom the first summer.

directions: Barely cover the fine seed and keep it lightly moist at room temperature until germination. Plant out in sharply drained soil in full sun and avoid overwatering. This selection carries larger, deeper lavender-blue flowers than the type; deadhead spent heads to prolong the summer display.

planting.note: Sow indoors in late winter or direct-sow after frost danger passes.

directions: Press the seed onto the surface of a gritty mix and cover only lightly, keeping it just moist until it germinates. Set plants in full sun in poor, well-drained soil where they will tolerate drought once established. The flowers are white with a contrasting deep purple-blue eye; snip stems in the morning for long-lasting cut or dried displays.

planting.note: Begin indoors before the last frost, or division of established clumps in spring.

directions: Surface-sow and lightly cover the seed, then keep it evenly moist until seedlings emerge in two to three weeks. Grow in full sun in lean, sharply drained ground and water sparingly. This white-flowered form produces papery ivory blooms all summer that dry well for everlasting arrangements.

planting.note: Start indoors in late winter for a compact plant that flowers its first year.

directions: Barely cover the seed and germinate warm, transplanting once seedlings have a few true leaves. This selection is more compact and uniform than the wild species, making it tidy at the front of a sunny, dry border. Expect a steady flush of blue daisy-like blooms from early summer; deadhead to keep them coming.

planting.note: Sow indoors before the last frost for compact plants that bloom the first summer.

directions: Press seed onto the surface, cover lightly, and keep warm and just moist until it sprouts. Plant into full sun in fast-draining, unfertile soil and let it dry between waterings. This dwarf white counterpart offers clean ivory flowers on short, sturdy stems ideal for tight sunny spaces.

planting.note: Start in pots for the rock garden and set out after frost; benefits from a cool spell before sowing.

directions: Sow into a very gritty mix and cover only lightly, keeping cool and just moist until germination. This dwarf Spanish species forms a tight rosette suited to troughs, scree, and rock gardens with sharp drainage. Short-stemmed yellow flowers appear over the low cushion in late spring and summer; avoid winter wet, which is its main enemy.

planting.note: Direct-sow into warm, well-drained soil around the last frost date.

directions: Scatter seed on warm, gritty soil and rake in lightly, thinning seedlings once they establish. Grow this Mediterranean annual in full sun with minimal water, as it thrives in poor dry ground. Small clear-yellow flowers with papery bracts open through summer and self-sow readily in mild sites.

planting.note: Set out once hard frost has passed and soil has begun to warm.

directions: Plant with the crown at soil level in a sunny, well-drained bed and firm the soil around the roots. Keep evenly moist through the first season and feed lightly every few weeks during active growth. Deadhead spent clusters to keep the small yellow blooms coming from late spring until frost.

planting.note: Plant after the last spring frost in a spot with good air movement.

directions: Set this spreading miniature into rich, free-draining soil and let its lax canes trail over a wall or edge. Water at the base to keep foliage dry and reduce mildew. Blooms open soft white, aging to a cool green tint, and repeat all season with regular deadheading.

planting.note: Transplant in spring once the ground can be worked easily.

directions: Dig a hole twice the width of the root ball and settle the plant so the roots sit just below the surface. Mulch lightly and give consistent water while it establishes. The tidy plant carries bright orange-scarlet buds that open flat, flowering heavily from early summer through fall.

planting.note: Plant out in spring after frosts end.

directions: Place in full sun with the graft or crown at grade and backfill with amended soil. Keep the root zone moist but never waterlogged. Clusters of small semi-double white blooms with yellow stamens open in flushes all season and carry a light honey scent.

planting.note: Set out in spring once the risk of frost is over.

directions: Plant this rounded mini-shrub in a sunny bed or large container with rich, draining mix. Water deeply and feed monthly through the growing season. Big cascading trusses of white pompon blooms appear from late spring to frost and need only a shear to tidy them.

planting.note: Best planted in spring; ideal for hanging baskets.

directions: Grow in a basket or at a bed's edge so the arching canes can spill downward. Keep the soil moist and site where the strong fragrance can be enjoyed. Fragrant lavender-purple clusters bloom repeatedly through the season with steady deadheading.

planting.note: Transplant in spring into a sunny, well-drained bed.

directions: Set the plant with its crown at soil level and give it room, as this is a tall, upright miniature. Keep evenly watered and feed regularly for best flowering. Perfectly formed apricot-blend buds open on long stems, making it a favorite for exhibition and cutting.

planting.note: Plant in spring after frost danger has passed.

directions: Dig in an amended, well-drained spot and firm soil gently around the roots. Water consistently and remove spent flowers to encourage rebloom. High-centered light pink blooms flushed with salmon open in classic form throughout the growing season.

planting.note: Set out in spring; noted for disease resistance.

directions: Plant in full sun with the crown at grade and mulch to conserve moisture. This tough, blackspot-resistant selection needs little spraying and thrives with basic watering and feeding. Single bright yellow flowers cover the bushy plant from late spring to hard frost.

planting.note: Transplant in spring into full sun.

directions: Place in a bright, well-drained spot and keep the root zone evenly moist. Feed lightly through summer and deadhead to prolong bloom. Buds open yellow and blush to red at the petal edges as they age, giving a multicolor effect on one plant.

planting.note: Plant in spring after the last frost.

directions: Set into rich, draining soil in full sun and firm around the roots. Keep watered and remove faded blooms to keep the show going. White petals edged in red give a distinctive picotee look, flowering in flushes across the season.

planting.note: Plant in spring; train up a support or let it trail.

directions: Grow at a wall, over a basket, or as a ground cover, tying young canes to guide them. Keep the soil moist and feed during flushes of growth. Masses of tiny dark red blooms cascade over the pliable canes and repeat from early summer to frost.

planting.note: Plant in spring for cover the same season; space for a solid carpet.

directions: Dig a wide hole, set the plant at grade, and mulch to smother weeds while it fills in. Once established it tolerates heat and dry spells with little care. Glossy foliage carries sprays of pink single blooms nonstop from late spring until frost with no need to deadhead.

planting.note: Plant in spring and mulch well to suppress weeds.

directions: Space plants so their spreading canes knit together into a low mound of glossy green. Water through the first year, then it needs little supplemental care. Clean white flowers cover the plant in continuous waves and shed their petals cleanly without deadheading.

planting.note: Plant in spring; tolerant of poorer soils and light shade.

directions: Set the plant at grade in almost any decent soil and water while it settles in. This tough, low-care rose shrugs off heat and neglect once established. Large trusses of small double pink rosettes appear a little later than most roses but then bloom heavily until frost.

planting.note: Plant in spring; nearly thornless and good for containers.

directions: Plant in a sunny to lightly shaded spot with the crown at soil level. Its nearly smooth canes make it easy to handle near paths and pots. Fragrant blush-white blooms open from pink-tinted buds and repeat reliably all season.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a warm, sunny position.

directions: Dig a generous hole and set the plant at grade in fertile, draining soil. Keep watered while it establishes, then feed after each flush. Small, perfectly formed apricot-yellow buds open to buff-cream blooms in generous clusters through the season.

planting.note: Plant in spring; compact enough for edging and pots.

directions: Plant this low, mounded rose at the front of a border or in a container with the crown at grade. Water regularly and shear lightly to keep it neat. Red-tinged buds open to pompon-like white blooms borne in dense clusters from early summer to frost.

planting.note: Plant in spring; buds may ball in cool wet weather.

directions: Site in full sun with good air movement to help the full buds open cleanly. Water at the base and feed after each bloom cycle. Very double, quartered blooms open pale pink to cream with a strong old-rose fragrance and repeat well in warm climates.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a sunny bed or pot.

directions: Plant the compact bush at grade in fertile soil and water while it establishes. Deadhead clusters and feed lightly for continuous flowering. Rounded, cupped salmon-orange blooms are carried in tight clusters and are popular as a pot rose.

planting.note: Plant in spring; low and tidy for edging.

directions: Set this dwarf polyantha at grade in a sunny front-of-border spot and keep it watered. Nearly thornless canes make it easy to work around. Big clusters of small rose-pink blooms nearly hide the foliage and repeat through the season.

common_name: Reine des Violettes Hybrid Perpetual Rose

planting.note: Plant bare-root in early spring or fall in mild areas.

directions: Plant in deep, rich soil with the graft just below the surface and mulch well. Feed generously, as hybrid perpetuals reward heavy feeding with repeat bloom. Very double, quartered violet-purple flowers carry a strong old-rose scent and repeat after the main early-summer flush.

planting.note: Plant in early spring or in fall where winters are mild.

directions: Dig a deep hole in fertile soil and set the plant with its bud union near grade. Water deeply and feed after each flush to encourage rebloom. Large, flat, quartered rose-pink blooms with a green button eye are richly fragrant and appear repeatedly through the season.

planting.note: Plant in early spring in deep, fertile ground.

directions: Plant in rich soil with strong stakes, as the very large blooms can nod on their stems. Feed heavily and water deeply for the biggest flowers. Enormous, full pink blooms up to five inches across are among the largest of the old roses and repeat after the first flush.

common_name: Frau Karl Druschki Hybrid Perpetual Rose

planting.note: Plant in early spring; can be trained as a short climber.

directions: Set in deep, well-worked soil and mulch to keep roots cool and moist. Its long, vigorous canes can be pegged down or tied to a support to boost bloom. Pure white high-centered blooms open from pink-tinted buds and repeat, though the flowers carry little scent.

planting.note: Plant in early spring for a striped-flower feature.

directions: Plant in fertile, draining soil with the bud union near grade and mulch well. Feed after each flush to keep the repeat blooms coming. Cupped pink flowers boldly striped and splashed with crimson are fragrant and one of the best repeating striped old roses.

common_name: Mrs. John Laing Hybrid Perpetual Rose

planting.note: Plant in early spring; reliable repeat bloomer.

directions: Set the plant in rich soil with its bud union near the surface and water in well. Feed regularly for one of the most dependable repeat displays among old roses. Cupped, soft satin-pink blooms on strong upright stems are fragrant and good for cutting.

planting.note: Plant in early spring in deep soil.

directions: Plant in fertile, well-drained soil and mulch to conserve moisture. Feed after the first flush and prune out old wood to keep the bush vigorous. Fragrant bright crimson blooms open from long-lasting buds and give a lighter repeat in fall.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a warm, sheltered spot; best in mild climates.

directions: Choose a warm, sheltered site and plant with the crown at grade in free-draining soil. Prune lightly, as teas resent hard cutting, and feed after each flush. Nodding apricot-yellow blooms open from long buds and repeat freely, carrying a distinctive tea fragrance.

planting.note: Plant in spring; thrives in heat and humidity.

directions: Plant in warm, well-drained soil with the crown at grade and water while establishing. This vigorous tea shrugs off heat and blooms almost continuously in mild regions. Cupped, nodding soft-pink blooms open in nonstop succession with a light, sweet fragrance.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a warm site; excellent Southern garden rose.

directions: Plant with the crown at grade in fertile, draining soil and give it a warm position. Prune lightly and feed after each flush of bloom. High-centered blooms of soft pink flushed deeper at the edges open from elegant buds and repeat all season.

planting.note: Plant in spring; nearly thornless and heat-loving.

directions: Set into warm, well-drained soil with the crown at grade and keep watered while establishing. The nearly smooth canes make it easy to handle in a mixed border. Yellow blooms blush pink and crimson at the edges in cool weather, repeating over a long season.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a warm, sunny site.

directions: Plant with the crown at grade in fertile, free-draining soil and water while it settles. Prune lightly and feed after each bloom cycle for continuous flowers. Full blooms of pale yellow edged in rose open from shapely buds and repeat generously in warm climates.

planting.note: Plant in spring against a warm wall or fence.

directions: Plant at the foot of a support and tie in the pliable canes to build a framework. Keep watered and feed after the first flush to fuel the long rebloom. Clusters of small semi-double blush-pink blooms carry a spicy clove scent and repeat until frost.

planting.note: Plant in spring against a warm support; the original Noisette.

directions: Set the plant beside a fence or pillar and tie in new canes as they lengthen. Water and feed through summer to keep the repeat clusters coming. Large trusses of small pink semi-double blooms are lightly scented and flower over a long season.

planting.note: Plant in spring; tolerates a north or shaded wall.

directions: Plant at the base of a large wall or arch and tie the vigorous canes to a framework. Unusually tolerant, it performs even on a cooler shaded aspect. Loose, cupped blush-white blooms are strongly fragrant and repeat from early summer to late autumn.

planting.note: Plant in spring against a warm, sunny wall.

directions: Plant beside a sturdy support and tie in the strong canes to cover it. Feed and water through the season to sustain repeat flowering. Loosely double buff-yellow to golden blooms are borne freely and carry a soft tea fragrance.

planting.note: Plant in spring on a warm wall or pillar.

directions: Set at the base of a support and train the flexible canes over it. Water and feed while establishing, then it flowers with little fuss. Loose apricot-orange blooms are produced almost continuously in warm climates with a light fragrance.

planting.note: Plant in spring on a warm, sheltered wall.

directions: Plant against a large support and tie in the vigorous canes as they extend. Feed and water to fuel the repeat bloom on this big climber. Nodding, quartered pale-lemon to white blooms hang gracefully and carry a strong tea fragrance.

planting.note: Plant in early spring; large shrub needing room.

directions: Give this big arching shrub plenty of space and plant in well-drained soil. Prune only to shape after flowering, as it blooms on older wood. Single scarlet-red flowers appear once in early summer and are followed by large flagon-shaped orange-red hips.

planting.note: Plant in spring on a large warm wall; thornless and very vigorous.

directions: Plant against a very large, sturdy support in warm-climate gardens and guide the thornless canes upward. Prune only after flowering, since blooms form on old wood. Small double butter-yellow flowers smother the plant in a single spectacular spring flush.

planting.note: Plant in early spring; vigorous for hedging and hips.

directions: Plant in well-drained soil for an informal hedge or wildlife planting and water while establishing. Its arching thorny canes make a dense barrier with little maintenance. Pale pink single blooms in early summer give way to abundant oval red hips prized for jelly and tea.

planting.note: Plant in spring; trails as a groundcover or over low walls.

directions: Plant at grade and let the long trailing canes spread to cover the ground or drape a wall. Water while establishing, then it asks for little care. Single pearly pink-white blooms are carried in trusses along the canes in a heavy early-summer flush with some later bloom.

planting.note: Plant in spring; spreads to cover banks and edges.

directions: Set plants so the trailing canes can knit together over a bank or spill from a wall. Water while establishing and shear lightly to renew growth. Clusters of small double blooms open creamy white with a pink tinge and repeat well through the season.

planting.note: Plant in spring; roots along trailing canes to cover ground.

directions: Plant on a bank or open bed where the vigorous prostrate canes can spread and root as they run. Very hardy and tough, it needs almost no care once established. Single pink blooms with white centers appear in one strong early-summer flush over glossy rugose foliage.

planting.note: Plant in spring; low-care landscape groundcover.

directions: Plant in a well-drained bed or slope where the arching canes can spread into a mound. Once established it tolerates heat and drought with minimal care. Large sprays of small scarlet-red blooms cover the plant repeatedly from summer to frost.

planting.note: Plant in spring; very hardy low-maintenance shrub.

directions: Plant at grade in a sunny, well-drained bed and mulch to conserve moisture. This durable landscape rose needs no spraying and little pruning. Single deep-pink blooms with white eyes flower in abundant clusters all season and set red hips in fall.

planting.note: Plant in spring after the last frost in full sun.

directions: Set into a sunny, well-drained bed or container with the crown at grade. Keep evenly moist and feed lightly through the season. Bright, non-fading yellow blooms with a light spicy scent appear on a compact, disease-resistant plant from spring to frost.

planting.note: Plant in spring; compact, award-winning landscape rose.

directions: Plant at grade in a sunny bed and mulch to keep down weeds while it spreads. This low, dense rose is very disease-resistant and needs little upkeep. Sprays of small semi-double white blooms cover the glossy mound continuously from summer to frost.

planting.note: Plant in spring once soil is workable and frost risk is fading.

directions: Choose a bright, open site and enrich the planting hole with compost before setting the roots. Deep golden-yellow, strongly fragrant blooms hold their color well and recur reliably through the season. Prune in late winter to outward-facing buds and deadhead to keep new flushes coming.

planting.note: Set out in early spring in a sunny bed.

directions: Plant this free-flowering floribunda where its large, camellia-like trusses of soft pink can be admired up close. Blooms come in generous sprays that repeat quickly after deadheading. Prune moderately each late winter and feed through the growing season for the best display.

planting.note: Plant after hard frosts end for strong root establishment.

directions: Site this unusual floribunda in full sun to bring out its smoky, chocolate-orange coloring. The lightly fragrant blooms appear in clusters on an upright, bushy plant through the warm months. Deadhead spent flowers and prune in late winter to keep growth vigorous and well-shaped.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a warm, sunny location.

directions: Plant this striking floribunda where its burgundy-and-cream striped, spicy-scented blooms can be enjoyed. No two flowers are marked exactly alike, and they recur in flushes all season. Deadhead to encourage rebloom and prune in late winter to maintain a rounded form.

planting.note: Set out once the soil warms in spring.

directions: Give this disease-resistant floribunda full sun and average, well-drained soil. Ruffled blooms in blended mango, peach, and apricot cover the rounded bush in repeating waves. Deadhead lightly and prune in late winter; it needs little fuss to perform well.

planting.note: Plant in spring in fertile, well-drained soil.

directions: Plant this prized floribunda where its exceptional fragrance can be appreciated near a path or seating area. Pearly white blooms flushed with pink open from shapely buds throughout the season. Deadhead often and prune moderately in late winter to keep the plant blooming freely.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a sunny, sheltered bed.

directions: Set this fragrant floribunda in full sun with rich, well-drained soil. Yellow blooms edged in red carry a strong, sweet scent and repeat well from summer to autumn. Deadhead spent flowers and prune in late winter to keep the bush compact and productive.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a sunny, well-drained spot.

directions: Plant this easy-care shrub in full sun where its yellow buds open to lightly scented, softening blooms. The flowers self-clean and repeat all season with no deadheading required. Prune by about one-third in late winter to keep the plant dense and vigorous.

planting.note: Plant near a support in spring or during dormancy.

directions: Train this graceful English climber against a wall, arch, or pillar in a sunny spot. Soft pink, cupped blooms carry a fragrance blending old rose, musk, and myrrh, followed by hips if left unpruned. Tie in the long canes horizontally to encourage flowering along their length.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a sheltered, sunny position.

directions: Site this upright English rose where its powerful myrrh fragrance can be enjoyed near a doorway or seat. Warm apricot buds open to soft cream-yellow, chalice-shaped blooms that repeat well. Prune in late winter and it can also be trained as a short climber in mild areas.

planting.note: Plant in spring in fertile, sunny ground.

directions: Plant this colorful English rose in full sun where the bronze new foliage sets off the flowers. Tangerine-orange buds open to rich orange blooms with a strong fruity fragrance and repeat well. Prune by a third in late winter and deadhead to sustain the display.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a sunny, enriched bed.

directions: Give this rounded English rose full sun and average, fertile soil. Large, domed rosettes of salmon-pink with gold undertones nod attractively and carry a fresh fruity fragrance. Prune in late winter and feed after each flush to keep it blooming into autumn.

planting.note: Plant in spring; can be grown as a shrub or short climber.

directions: Plant this vigorous English rose in full sun as a large shrub or train it upward as a climber. Rich yellow centers fade to paler outer petals, giving a soft two-tone effect with a good fragrance. Prune according to the form you want in late winter and tie in canes if growing on a support.

planting.note: Plant in spring in fertile, sunny soil.

directions: Set this upright English rose in full sun where its deep pink blooms hold their color well. Flowers open with many narrow petals and carry a light tea fragrance, repeating strongly all season. Prune in late winter and deadhead to maintain continuous bloom on the compact bush.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a sunny, enriched bed.

directions: Plant this classic English rose in full sun with room to develop its arching, bushy shape. Soft shell-pink, cupped blooms carry a lovely fragrance with lemon and honey notes and repeat well. Prune moderately in late winter and feed after the first flush for a strong second display.

planting.note: Plant in spring in average, well-drained soil.

directions: This dependable, bushy English rose grows easily in full sun and average soil. Loose, rose-pink blooms with a light old-rose fragrance appear early and repeat generously all season. Prune by about one-third in late winter to keep the well-branched plant tidy and floriferous.

planting.note: Plant in spring as a shrub or short climber.

directions: Grow this versatile English rose in full sun as a rounded shrub or trained onto a low support. Soft yellow, flat rosettes with paler edges carry a fresh fragrance and repeat reliably. Prune to suit the chosen form in late winter and deadhead through the season.

planting.note: Plant near a sturdy support in spring or when dormant.

directions: Train this near-thornless English climber up a wall, arch, or pillar in full sun. Large, full rosettes of warm pink with paler outer petals appear in abundance and repeat through the season. Tie canes in horizontally to maximize flowering and prune lightly after the main flush.

planting.note: Plant in spring as a shrub or trained on a low support.

directions: Plant this arching English rose in full sun where its rich crimson blooms stand out. Cupped, loosely double flowers carry a strong old-rose fragrance and repeat through the season. Prune in late winter and, if grown on a support, tie in the flexible canes.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a sunny bed or border.

directions: Plant this neat, upright English rose in full sun where its tidy habit suits formal borders. Soft apricot-pink, cupped blooms hold a very upright poise and carry a light myrrh fragrance. Prune in late winter and deadhead to keep the compact plant blooming into autumn.

planting.note: Plant in spring in average, well-drained soil.

directions: Grow this healthy, bushy English rose in full sun for a rounded, well-clothed shrub. Pure pink, informal rosettes carry a classic old-rose fragrance and repeat freely all season. Prune by a third in late winter and remove spent blooms to encourage new flushes.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a sunny, fertile bed.

directions: Plant this rounded English rose in full sun with average, well-prepared soil. Deep pink, many-petaled rosettes carry a good old-rose fragrance with a raspberry note and repeat well. Prune in late winter and deadhead through summer to maintain continuous bloom.

planting.note: Plant near a support in spring; tolerates a shadier wall.

directions: Grow this thornless Bourbon climber on a wall or arch, even a partly shaded one, in enriched soil. Cerise-pink, semi-double blooms carry a strong sweet fragrance and repeat from summer into autumn. Tie in canes as they grow and watch for mildew where air movement is poor.

planting.note: Plant against a support in spring or when dormant.

directions: Train this popular climber on a trellis or wall in full sun for its distinctive bicolor blooms. Creamy petals edged in rosy pink open from shapely buds and repeat through the season. Tie in canes horizontally to boost flowering and prune lightly after the main flush.

planting.note: Plant near a warm wall or fence in spring.

directions: Grow this old-style climber against a sunny, sheltered wall in fertile soil. Flat, quartered creamy-white blooms are packed with petals and carry a strong tea fragrance, repeating through the season. Tie in the pliable canes and prune lightly to keep the framework open.

planting.note: Plant beside a wall or arch in spring; tolerates some shade.

directions: Train this vigorous Noisette climber on a large wall or into a tree, even on a north-facing aspect. Blush-white, loosely double blooms carry a warm fragrance and repeat generously through the season. Tie in the long canes and prune only to guide and thin the framework.

planting.note: Plant in spring as a large shrub or short climber.

directions: Give this vigorous, hardy shrub full sun and space to grow tall, or train it as a pillar rose. Ruffled apricot-orange blooms are strongly fragrant and repeat reliably from summer to frost. Prune in late winter to shape and tie in canes if growing on a support.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a warm, sunny, well-drained spot.

directions: Plant this classic Bourbon in a warm, sunny position where rain is less likely to spoil the blooms. Flat, quartered pale-pink flowers carry a rich fragrance and repeat well in dry, settled weather. Prune lightly in late winter and remove damaged blooms promptly in wet spells.

planting.note: Plant in spring in fertile, sunny ground.

directions: Grow this graceful Bourbon in full sun and support its arching canes on a low frame if needed. Warm rose-pink, camellia-like blooms are richly fragrant and repeat reliably through the season. Prune lightly in late winter and deadhead to keep the flushes coming.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a warm, sunny position.

directions: Give this large China rose full sun and shelter, where it can reach shrub or short-climber size in mild areas. Single blooms open buff-yellow and change through pink to crimson, giving a multicolored effect over a long season. Prune lightly in late winter and it needs little further care.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a warm, sunny bed.

directions: Grow this curiosity in full sun where its unusual blooms can be admired up close. The flowers have no true petals, instead forming clusters of narrow green sepals that age to bronze and last well when cut. Prune lightly in late winter; it is easy to grow and long-flowering.

planting.note: Plant in spring; tolerates poor, sandy, and coastal soils.

directions: Plant this extremely hardy rugosa in sun as an informal hedge or in tough coastal and roadside sites. Clove-scented, mauve-purple double blooms repeat all season and are followed by large red hips. Its wrinkled foliage resists disease; simply remove old canes at the base in late winter.

planting.note: Plant in spring; extremely cold-tolerant and adaptable.

directions: Choose this exceptionally hardy rugosa hybrid for cold-climate gardens and informal hedges. Fragrant, ruffled lilac-pink blooms appear early and repeat, on nearly thornless canes that redden attractively in winter. Remove the oldest canes at the base in late winter to renew the shrub.

planting.note: Plant in spring; thrives in poor and sandy soils.

directions: Plant this large, tough rugosa in sun as a fragrant hedge or specimen in difficult soils. Deep wine-crimson, semi-double blooms carry a powerful clove scent and repeat all season. The healthy, wrinkled foliage needs little care beyond removing old canes at the base in late winter.

planting.note: Set out in spring or early fall once the ground is workable and frost danger has passed.

directions: Dig the planting hole as deep as the root ball and twice as wide, backfill with amended soil, and water deeply to settle the roots. Prune hard in late winter, since blooms form on new wood, and keep the soil evenly moist through the first two seasons. Large conical panicles open creamy white in midsummer and deepen to rich rose-pink as autumn approaches.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in a spot with good drainage and at least a half day of sun.

directions: Position this compact selection at the front of a border, setting it at the same depth it grew in the pot and firming the soil gently. It blooms on new growth, so cut stems back by about a third in early spring to encourage a dense mound of flowers. Dozens of small white panicles cover the plant from midsummer into fall.

planting.note: Plant in spring or early fall in moist, fertile soil that drains freely.

directions: Give this upright, glossy-leaved shrub room to reach its mature size and water it well while it establishes. Because flowers appear on current-season wood, prune in late winter to shape and to promote strong stems. Airy white panicles open in mid to late summer and are especially attractive to bees.

planting.note: Set out in spring or fall in a sunny to lightly shaded site with rich soil.

directions: Plant at the original soil line, mulch to conserve moisture, and keep young plants watered through dry spells. Cut back the previous year's growth in early spring to build a sturdy framework for the heavy flower heads. Broad white panicles open in summer, flush pink with cool weather, and can be dried for arrangements.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in full sun to part shade with even moisture.

directions: Dig a wide hole, plant level with the surrounding grade, and water thoroughly to remove air pockets. Prune in late winter before growth begins to keep the strong stems upright under the dense flower load. Solid, oval white panicles hold their color well through summer before taking on a pink cast in fall.

planting.note: Plant in spring or early fall in fertile, well-drained soil with good sun.

directions: Site this distinctive shrub where its open habit has room to spread, and keep it watered while roots establish. Prune lightly in late winter to shape without sacrificing the arching flower stems. Large sterile florets with propeller-shaped petals stand out among the smaller fertile flowers from midsummer onward.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in a bright spot with moist, well-drained soil.

directions: Plant at the depth it grew in the container and mulch to keep the roots cool and moist. Strong, upright stems hold the large flower heads without flopping, and a late-winter prune on the old wood keeps the plant vigorous. Cone-shaped blooms open white and mature to a deep strawberry-red from the base upward.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in full sun to light shade with steady moisture.

directions: Set the plant level with the ground, water deeply, and mulch to reduce moisture swings. Prune the prior year's stems back in early spring, as flowers come on new wood. Long panicles emerge white, shift to pink, and finish deep wine-red, with honey-scented flowers over a long season.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in well-drained soil with at least a half day of sun.

directions: Plant at grade, firm the soil, and keep the root zone moist through the establishment year. Because it blooms on new growth, cut it back in late winter to encourage plenty of the small flower heads. Dainty white panicles made of tiny florets cover the plant in midsummer and take on a soft pink tint in fall.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in fertile soil with good drainage and full sun.

directions: Dig a hole twice as wide as the root ball, plant level with the grade, and water in well. Prune in late winter to a low framework so the sturdy stems support the heavy heads. Dense panicles open white and quickly turn to the deepest red of any panicle hydrangea, holding color into autumn.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in a sunny site with moist, well-drained soil.

directions: Plant at the same depth it grew in the pot, water deeply, and mulch to conserve soil moisture. Cut stems back by about a third in early spring to keep the plant strong and blooming heavily on new wood. Full panicles open pure white and color up from the bottom to a rich pink-red, giving a two-tone effect.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in well-drained soil with full to part sun.

directions: Set this compact shrub at grade in a border or container and keep it watered during establishment. Prune in early spring, since bloom comes on the current year's stems, to maintain a tidy rounded form. Compact panicles open white and turn strawberry-pink, resembling the color of a fruit sundae.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in a sunny spot with fertile, moisture-retentive soil.

directions: Plant level with the surrounding soil, water in thoroughly, and mulch to hold moisture. Prune hard in late winter to build strong stems capable of holding the very large flower heads upright. Massive dense panicles open bright white in mid to late summer and take on a green cast as they age.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in well-drained soil with full sun to light shade.

directions: Dig a wide hole, plant at grade, and water deeply to settle the roots around the crown. Its strong, dark stems resist flopping, and a late-winter cutback keeps flowering vigorous on new wood. Upright white panicles hold their crisp color through the summer with only a slight aging tint.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in fertile, moist soil with good sun exposure.

directions: Plant at the original soil line, mulch, and keep the soil moist while the shrub gets established. Prune the previous season's growth in late winter to encourage large flower heads on the new stems. Long, pointed panicles open creamy green, turn white, and finish soft pink as the season cools.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in humus-rich, moist soil with morning sun and afternoon shade.

directions: Plant this dwarf mophead at grade in a sheltered bed or container and keep the soil consistently moist. It blooms on old wood, so prune only lightly right after flowering to avoid removing next year's buds. Small rounded flower heads appear in summer, coloring pink in alkaline soil and blue where the soil is acidic.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in rich, moist soil with protection from hot afternoon sun.

directions: Set the plant level with the soil surface, mulch generously, and water often during dry spells. Prune just after bloom, as flower buds form on the previous year's wood. Unusual cupped, lilac-like florets form rounded heads in summer that shade from pink to blue depending on soil pH.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in moist, well-drained soil with part sun.

directions: Plant at grade in a spot sheltered from midday heat and keep the roots cool with mulch and regular water. Prune only spent flowers and dead wood after bloom to protect the old-wood buds. Deep rosy-red mophead flowers hold their bold color even in acidic soils where other cultivars turn blue.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in fertile, moist soil with morning sun.

directions: Plant level with the grade, mulch well, and provide steady moisture through the growing season. Prune lightly after flowering since the buds are set on old wood the year before. Sturdy mophead heads of deep pink to purple flowers appear in summer over glossy dark foliage.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in rich, moist soil shaded from harsh afternoon sun.

directions: Set the plant at grade, mulch to keep roots cool, and water regularly to prevent wilting. Prune just after the flowers fade, taking care not to remove next year's buds on the old wood. Red stems carry lacecap flowers that age from white to pink and deep red, and the foliage reddens in fall.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in moist, well-drained soil with part shade.

directions: Plant at grade, mulch, and keep the soil evenly moist, especially in the first two seasons. This reblooming type flowers on both old and new wood, so prune only to remove dead stems and shape lightly. Large mophead heads bloom from early summer onward, turning blue in acidic soil or pink in alkaline soil.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in humus-rich, moist soil with morning sun.

directions: Plant level with the ground, mulch the root zone, and water consistently during dry weather. Because it blooms on new as well as old wood, it recovers from winter dieback and needs only light pruning. Rounded mophead flowers appear over a long summer season, blue in acidic soil and pink in alkaline soil.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in fertile, moist soil with afternoon shade.

directions: Set the plant at grade, mulch to retain moisture, and water regularly through summer. Prune spent blooms after flowering, leaving old wood intact since it carries next year's buds. Large white mophead heads with a faint pink or blue eye open in summer and age gracefully to soft pink.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in moist, rich soil with part shade.

directions: Plant at grade, mulch, and keep the root zone consistently moist to avoid stress. Prune lightly after bloom to shape, preserving the old-wood buds that produce next season's flowers. Early, free-flowering mophead heads open rose-pink or clear blue depending on soil acidity.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in moist, well-drained soil with morning sun.

directions: Plant level with the grade, mulch well, and provide steady moisture during the growing season. Prune only after flowering, removing spent heads and leaving the old wood that bears next year's buds. Bold lacecap flowers open deep violet-blue in acidic soil or rich pink where the soil is alkaline.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in rich, moist soil with part shade.

directions: Set the plant at grade, mulch, and water regularly to keep the large leaves from wilting. This reblooming selection flowers on old and new wood, so prune only to tidy and remove dead stems. Big mophead heads bloom reliably even after cold winters, turning blue or pink with soil pH.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in moist, well-drained soil with morning sun.

directions: Plant this compact reblooming type at grade in a bed or large pot and keep it evenly watered. It flowers on both old and new wood, so only light shaping or deadheading is needed. Vivid raspberry-red or neon-purple mophead heads appear from early summer to frost, shifting with soil pH.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in rich, moist soil with dappled or morning sun.

directions: Plant at grade, mulch to keep the roots cool, and water regularly during dry spells. This reblooming cultivar flowers on old and new wood, so prune only lightly to shape. Double florets are white edged in deep pink-red, forming full mophead heads that repeat through summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in moist, well-drained soil with morning sun and afternoon shade.

directions: Plant level with the grade, mulch, and keep the soil moist through the establishment period. Part of the reblooming Endless Summer group, it flowers on old and new wood and needs only light pruning. Sturdy red-purple stems hold large mophead heads that bloom from early summer to fall, blue or rose-pink by soil pH.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in rich, moist soil with part shade.

directions: Plant this compact selection at grade, mulch, and keep the soil consistently moist. It reblooms on old and new wood, so prune only lightly and remove spent heads to encourage more flowers. Frilly double white florets form both an outer lacecap ring and a central dome, staying white regardless of soil pH.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in moist, well-drained soil with morning sun.

directions: Plant at grade, mulch, and water regularly to keep the compact plant lush. Prune after flowering, leaving old wood since the buds form the previous year. Bright pink mophead heads open early and hold their color well even in acidic soils that turn other cultivars blue.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in moist, well-drained soil with part shade.

directions: Plant this hardy mountain type at grade, mulch, and keep the soil evenly moist. It reblooms on old and new wood and tolerates cold well, so prune only to tidy and remove dead stems. Semi-double lacecap flowers open from midsummer, ranging from pink to blue depending on soil acidity.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in humus-rich, moist soil with part shade.

directions: Set the plant at grade, mulch, and provide steady moisture, especially in warm weather. This reblooming mountain hydrangea flowers on old and new wood and needs only minimal pruning. Reblooming lacecap heads carry ruffled florets that turn vivid pink or blue with the soil pH.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in moist, well-drained soil with dappled shade.

directions: Plant at grade, mulch to conserve moisture, and water during dry periods. Prune only after flowering, since this species blooms on old wood. Delicate lacecap heads with serrated blue or lilac-pink florets appear in summer, and the foliage often colors well in fall.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in moist, well-drained soil with part shade.

directions: Plant level with the grade, mulch, and keep the soil moist to support the spreading habit. Prune spent flowers after bloom, leaving old wood intact for the next year's buds. Flat lacecap heads of blue fertile flowers ringed with pale sterile florets open in summer over this cold-hardy shrub.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in rich, moist soil with morning sun and afternoon shade.

directions: Plant at grade, mulch, and water regularly to keep this Japanese selection thriving. Prune lightly after flowering to preserve the old-wood flower buds. White lacecap florets are edged in crimson-pink, and the emerging foliage carries an attractive dark-red tint.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in moist, well-drained soil with part shade.

directions: Set the plant at grade, mulch, and keep the root zone moist during the growing season. Prune spent heads after bloom, taking care to leave the old wood that carries next season's flowers. Early lacecap heads open bright blue in acidic soil, and the leaves take on a red-purple tone in full sun.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in moist, well-drained soil with part shade.

directions: Plant at grade, mulch, and keep the soil moist while the shrub establishes. Because it blooms on new wood, cut the stems back in late winter to encourage fresh growth and abundant flowers. Double, star-shaped white florets form lacy heads that stay attractive for weeks, though stems may need light support.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in moist, well-drained soil with part shade.

directions: Plant level with the grade, mulch, and provide even moisture during dry spells. It flowers on new wood, so prune in late winter to promote sturdy stems that resist flopping. Domed lacecap heads of creamy sterile florets surround tiny fertile flowers, drawing pollinators through summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in moist, well-drained soil with full to part sun.

directions: Plant at grade, mulch, and water regularly through the first seasons. Cut the plant back hard in late winter, since it blooms on new wood and rebounds vigorously. Two-toned heads open deep ruby-red and silvery-pink, blooming from summer to frost on strong upright stems.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in moist, well-drained soil with full to part sun.

directions: Plant this compact selection at grade, mulch, and keep the soil evenly moist. Cut it back in late winter to promote a dense, well-branched mound that flowers on new wood. Sturdy stems hold rounded mauve-pink heads from summer into fall without flopping.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in moist, well-drained soil with morning sun for best leaf color.

directions: Plant at grade, mulch, and water regularly while the shrub establishes. Prune only after flowering, since oakleaf types bloom on old wood. Golden-yellow oak-shaped leaves emerge in spring and turn red in fall, while upright white panicles open in early summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in moist, well-drained soil with part sun.

directions: Plant level with the grade, mulch, and provide even moisture during the first two seasons. Prune after bloom only, protecting the old wood that carries next year's flowers. Compact white panicles age to deep rosy-red, and the bold oak-shaped foliage turns burgundy in fall.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in moist, well-drained soil with part shade.

directions: Plant at grade, mulch, and keep the soil moist while the roots take hold. Prune spent flowers after bloom, since this type flowers on old wood. Unusually large, dense white panicles are so heavy they arch the branches, and the oak-shaped leaves color richly in autumn.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall at the base of a wall or tree in moist, well-drained soil with part shade.

directions: Plant near a sturdy support and angle the young stems toward it, since the vine climbs by clinging aerial rootlets and is slow to start. Keep the roots cool and moist with mulch and be patient through the first few years while it establishes. Fragrant white lacecap flowers appear in early summer against green leaves broadly margined in creamy yellow.

planting.note: Set out in cool early spring or early fall so roots settle before summer heat.

directions: Plant the root ball slightly high in acidic, humus-rich soil, keeping the crown at or just above grade to avoid rot. Mulch with pine bark or needles and water deeply through dry spells. Trusses of white flowers with faint yellow throats open in late spring on this compact, cold-tolerant hybrid.

planting.note: Best moved in spring or early fall into dappled shade.

directions: Give this vigorous old hybrid room and acidic, well-drained soil enriched with organic matter. Shelter it from harsh afternoon sun and drying wind, and keep the shallow roots evenly moist under mulch. White bell-shaped flowers marked with a bold blackish-purple blotch appear in late spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in a spot with morning sun and afternoon shade.

directions: Work peat or composted bark into acidic soil and set the plant high to keep its fibrous roots near the surface. Mulch generously and never let the root zone dry out during bloom. Large conical trusses of soft rose-pink flowers fade to blush as they age each spring.

planting.note: Set out in spring or early fall in filtered light.

directions: Choose acidic, moisture-retentive but well-drained soil and plant the crown slightly proud of the surface. Feed lightly after bloom with an acid formulation and mulch to insulate the shallow roots. Bright brick-red flowers cover this rounded, dense shrub in late spring.
